items.topic_thesis: Internal collapse and competitive collapse are distinct failure modes.
items.angle: Open on Writer survey: AI adoption is tearing companies apart in plain language, then show how it makes the deeper diagnosis legible.
items.hook: The most immediate AI threat in many companies may not be losing the market. It may be breaking the organization first.
items.timely_subject: Writer survey: AI adoption is tearing companies apart

publish_payload.topic_thesis: Internal collapse and competitive collapse are distinct failure modes.
publish_payload.angle: Open on Writer survey: AI adoption is tearing companies apart in plain language, then show how it makes the deeper diagnosis legible.
publish_payload.hook: Writer’s latest survey found a majority of executives saying AI adoption is pulling their company apart. Read that literally. Not “change is hard.” Power struggles, IT-business conflict, and employee resistance severe enough to feel like internal rupture.
publish_payload.body_text:
Writer’s latest survey found a majority of executives saying AI adoption is pulling their company apart. Read that literally. Not “change is hard.” Power struggles, IT-business conflict, and employee resistance severe enough to feel like internal rupture.

That makes one important distinction legible: internal collapse and competitive collapse are different failure modes.

Competitive collapse is when the market moves faster than you do. Internal collapse is when you try to keep up by burning the trust that lets the company learn. From the outside, both can look like urgency. Only one leaves the organization intact.

AI makes this easier to see because it changes status before it changes process. People are not only reacting to tools. They are reacting to what the tools do to authority, identity, and decision rights.

A serious operator should track both scoreboards. Don’t ask only whether adoption is fast enough. Ask whether the company can absorb the shock without turning truth into politics. If not, speed is not strategy. It is liquidation.
publish_payload.footer_text: The Dionysus Program is free. It covers the machinery behind posts like this — Run Time, Ritual Time, the anti-scapegoat, and how to make criticism cheaper than silence. dionysusprogram.com
publish_payload.final_text:
Writer’s latest survey found a majority of executives saying AI adoption is pulling their company apart. Read that literally. Not “change is hard.” Power struggles, IT-business conflict, and employee resistance severe enough to feel like internal rupture.

That makes one important distinction legible: internal collapse and competitive collapse are different failure modes.

Competitive collapse is when the market moves faster than you do. Internal collapse is when you try to keep up by burning the trust that lets the company learn. From the outside, both can look like urgency. Only one leaves the organization intact.

AI makes this easier to see because it changes status before it changes process. People are not only reacting to tools. They are reacting to what the tools do to authority, identity, and decision rights.

A serious operator should track both scoreboards. Don’t ask only whether adoption is fast enough. Ask whether the company can absorb the shock without turning truth into politics. If not, speed is not strategy. It is liquidation.

---

items.seed_topic_thesis: To found trust without building a cult, leaders must transgress, recognize, release, and submit.

x_publish_payload.text:
AI adoption is exposing a harder failure than falling behind: companies breaking themselves to move faster.

Markets can outrun you. But if speed burns trust, truth becomes politics and the org stops learning. That isn’t transformation. It’s liquidation.

items.topic_thesis: Living law has to submit itself to the same anti-scapegoat logic it imposes on everyone else.
items.angle: The interpretation is the point, not the quote itself.
items.hook: A note landed in my inbox that names a pattern I keep seeing. living law has to submit itself to the same anti-scapegoat logic it imposes on everyone else.

items.topic_thesis: The faster knowledge changes, the more an organization needs a stable myth layer.
items.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now.
items.hook: The mistake leaders keep making is thinking the faster knowledge changes, the more an organization needs a stable myth layer.

items.topic_thesis: Apollo and Dionysus are complements, not substitutes: one optimizes the known system, the other governs death and rebirth.
items.angle: Make the pattern recognizable fast and show what drives it.
items.hook: You can tell a team is drifting into theater when apollo and dionysus are complements, not substitutes: one optimizes the known system, the other governs death and rebirth.
